EC0866: Wildfire-specific fine particulate matter and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy in the U.S. ECHO-wide cohort

Multivariate Analyses

Daniel Beene

March 31, 2026

1 Models

Models performed on full dataset; n = 21095

1.1 Unadjusted

1.2 Primary Adjusted

From AP: “Primary models will be adjusted for variables widely available across cohorts including maternal age, education, parity, race/ethnicity.”

  • Model: Generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) via glmer() from lme4
  • Outcome: Gestational hypertension (binary), modeled with a binomial family and logit link. Results are interpreted as odds ratios
  • Exposure: Each of 12 wildfire smoke metrics are modeled separately
  • Random effect: Random intercept for cohort (CohortID_UH3), accounting for clustering of participants within study cohorts
  • Covariates: Maternal age (splined), birthing parent educational attainment, parity, race/ethnicity, infant sex
  • Missing data: Models fit across 10 multiply imputed datasets via mice::with() and pooled using Rubin’s rules via mice::pool() Optimization: bobyqa optimizer with increased max iterations (maxfun = 2e5) and nAGQ = 0 for computational stability

1.2.1 Plot

1.3 Fully Adjusted

From AP: “Extended models will be additionally adjusted for covariates selected based on availability of harmonized variables across cohorts, including household size-adjusted income (or neighborhood deprivation index), maternal pre-pregnancy BMI, history of chronic hypertension, history of pre-eclampsia, pre-pregnancy diabetes, prenatal smoking.”

  • Model: Same parameters as primary adjusted model for exposure, outcome, cohort random intercept, handling missing data, and model optimization.
  • Covariates: Maternal age (splined), secular trend (year splines), census macro region, tobacco use, alcohol consumption, tract-level poverty, pre-pregnancy BMI, birthing parent educational attainment, parity, race/ethnicity, infant sex

1.3.1 Cohort Effects

library(performance)

fit_icc <- with(wf_imp_primary,
                glmer(as.formula(formula_str),
                      family  = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      control = glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa",
                                             optCtrl   = list(maxfun = 2e5)),
                      nAGQ = 0))
icc(fit_icc$analyses[[1]])
## # Intraclass Correlation Coefficient
## 
##     Adjusted ICC: 0.158
##   Unadjusted ICC: 0.146

1.3.2 Plot

2 Effect Measure Modification

2.1 Mean PM X Infant Sex

  • n male = 10837

  • n female = 10257

Effect Measure Modification by Infant Sex: Mean Smoke PM
Exposure Stratum OR 95% CI Low 95% CI High P-value P-heterogeneity
preg_smokePM_mean…1 Pregnancy mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Male 0.930 0.789 1.097 0.3909 0.7965
preg_smokePM_mean…2 Pregnancy mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Female 0.902 0.759 1.070 0.2371 0.7965
T1_smokePM_mean…3 T1 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Male 1.047 0.968 1.133 0.2483 0.2576
T1_smokePM_mean…4 T1 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Female 0.977 0.891 1.070 0.6162 0.2576
T2_smokePM_mean…5 T2 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Male 0.918 0.829 1.018 0.1036 0.4994
T2_smokePM_mean…6 T2 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Female 0.965 0.873 1.067 0.4850 0.4994
T3_smokePM_mean…7 T3 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Male 0.991 0.924 1.062 0.7890 0.5552
T3_smokePM_mean…8 T3 mean smoke PM, μg/m³ Female 0.961 0.893 1.034 0.2851 0.5552

2.1.1 Plot

Notes:

  • Baseline probability separation (M > F) consistent across all timeframes. Q for Cindy: how does this fit with literature about infant sex differences in HDP?
  • OR forest plot shows CIs broadly overlapping 1/each other — no strong EMM by sex on multiplicative scale
  • Probability curve slopes are flat and parallel for both infant sexes, suggesting that effect of smoke PM doesn’t differ meaningfully by infant sex
